figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Effect of solute trapping parameter A on the concentration profiles with v p = 0.32 m s -1 and W = 13.5 nm for (a) D s /D l = 0.1 and b = 1.0 and (b) D s /D l = 0.001 and b = 0.00001.

figure.head: vFIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. (a) Comparison of the partition coefficient as a function of velocity obtained from phase field simulations for different values of parameter A. (b) Comparison of the interface temperature as function of velocity computed for different values of A. The solid lines represent the predictionfrom the CGM which are provided for the sake of comparison. W is fixed in the simulations and chosen to be 13.5 nm and the value of b is selected as 1 . In addition to trapping, A controls the solute drag in the system.

figure.head: vvFIG. 5 .vFIG. 6 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 5. Comparison of the partition coefficient from the phase field asymptotics obtained from the numerical solution of Eq. (106) with A, b and W as a fitting parameter and (a) the experimental results from Kittl et al. for Si-9 %As [60] and (b) Molecular Dynamics data for Ni-5 %Cu [61].

figure.head: v
figure.figDesc: FIG. 7. (a) Comparison of the present with previously developed phase field models and the allowable interface widths that emulates the experimental data from Kittl et al. [60] in Si-9 % As and (b) Comparison of the interface temperature from different phase field models.

figure.head: Fig. 5 (
figure.figDesc: b) represents the fit from solution of Eq. (106) to the MD simulation data on Ni-5 %Cu from Yang et al.[61] in the velocity range of v = 0 m s -1 to 2 m s -1 . In Fig.5(b) A = 1.8, b = 0.00001 and W = 7.8 nm has been chosen to best fit the data from the MD simulation. Once an estimate of the model parameters are derived, numerical simulations are performed to check the validity of the asymptotic expressions. A comparison of the prediction of Eq. (106) and numerical results from the phase field model for different combinations of A, b and W are shown in Fig.6(a) for the system Si-9 %As and for the Ni-5 %Cu system in Fig.6(b). The results for the phase field model are within 15% of the prediction of the analytical expression. At higher velocities, the choice of W is restricted by the requirement to resolve the diffusion length which is given by the ratio of interface diffusivity and pulling speed. It is evident from Fig.6that the interface width W can be increased without changing the partition coefficient by simultaneously tuning the values of parameters A and b. It is to be noted that the choice of A, b and W should also be chosen to capture the appropriate (measured experimentally or through atomistic simulations) velocity-dependent interface temperature.

figure.head: FIG. 8 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 8. Solute profiles exhibiting solute bands corresponding to (a) t = 1.92 × 10 -4 sec, (b) t = 2.56 × 10 -4 sec, (c) t = 3.84 × 10 -4 sec and (d) t = 5.12 × 10 -4 sec. Time periodic oscillations of the (e) interface velocity and (f) interface temperature. (h) Variation of oscillation wavelength with the temperature gradient.

(a). Not all phase field models have the same degree of freedom in tuning the interface temperature. The interface temperature can be controlled in the present model by the parameters A, b and W . Different choice of the model parameters gives rise to distinct curves as shown in Fig.7(b). The high velocity asymptotics of the WBM model predicts the interface temperature of the form given by Eq. (105) with partial drag (D = 24/35) embedded in the model. The interface temperature was only presented until velocity of 0.2 m s -1 in the study of Pinomaa et al. [44] and hence not included here. Different interface temperatures for different models indicate that the solute drag is sensitive to the specifics of the model construction. We remark that the interface temperature can be made to fall on the high-velocity asymptotic solution by appropriately choosing the model parameters A = 1.4, b = 0.00001 and W to 60.75 nm without appreciably changing the k v curve as shown earlier in Fig.(6). However, interface temperature alone cannot be changed without simultaneously changing k v if W is altered in the WBM and KKS model. Thus, while the model by Ahmad et al. is restricted by the choice of W = 3.5 nm to reproduce the experimental k v curve, the present model allows to increase the interface width by 60.75 nm to emulate the same k v and interface temperature curve.

With the choice of the source terms S s = S l = A(c l,0c s,0 ) as deduced earlier, the diffusion potential jump can simplified to be

which is similar to the Eq. ( 98) derived for the case where we had assumed µ s = µ l with the exception that lowest order phase concentrations (and the thermodynamic factors ∂c s,0 /∂µ s and ∂c l,0 /∂µ l ) are dependent on the normal co-ordinate system η i.e. c s,0 = c s,0 (η), c l,0 = c l,0 (η) and cannot be taken out of the integral to obtain closed form expressions. However, it is clear that the parameter A can still be tuned to control partitioning.

As discussed in the next section, a numerical comparison of the two models suggest that the steady state diffusion potential in both the models (Fig. 2)) suggests that the difference between the two models depend on the value of A. The agreement between the predictions of the two models is better at higher values of A within the entire velocity range considered.

Even with lower values of A (for instance A = 0.1) the agreement is good until velocities of about 0.1 m/s. A good agreement between both the models motivates us to utilize the same interpolation functions to negate the Kapitza jump (i.e. satisfy G -= G + ).
The next order φ and c equations are analytically not tractable. Hence no information regarding the correction to the Gibbs-Thomson and mass-conservation conditions are available.

div.head: V. COMPARATIVE STUDY OF BOTH THE MODELS
